text.content: to build a resilient & flexible system, you need iteration. it’s inevitable. a system can only prove its value when exposed to multiple different contexts. if it fails to adapt to a new context, it’s not a great system yet. as you iterate on multiple contexts, the system will progressively adapt to cover more edge cases, and patterns will start to emerge naturally. this takes time, effort and refactoring – you have to put in the work & not be afraid to break things in the process

text.content: sometimes I wonder if value-for-value was the main pricing model. i.e. pay what you want – buyer sets the price instead of seller. if the value is created through the lens of the customer – would this push businesses to offer better products & services? it’s also really common for a buyer to overrate the value of something right after experiencing it, or using it for the first time. this means profit for the seller – win-win situation

text.content: folder-based structures are possibly the most inefficient way to organize digital knowledge. expensive to navigate, as you need to go through each & every layer. risk of duplication, as a file can exist in multiple folders with no global visibility. mentally taxing to work out when working with other people – everyone conceptualizes information layers differently. flat structures may seem chaotic at first, but they’re the way to go. auto-generated keywords & taxonomies can help fast discovery, and provide a passive way to organize knowledge with little active input required. only input needed is a search query – everything else works behind the scenes. a robust, well-implemented search will beat folder-based structures any day of the week

text.content: realizing i spent a lot of time learning music the wrong way. a lot of teaching is focused on giving you highly applicable snippets: cool chords, pentatonic scales, neo-soul progressions. you memorize these snippets for one song, one context; and you’re unable export that knowledge elsewhere. this is the wrong way. the right way is conceptual relations first: how each chord functions in a progression, how each note functions in a chord. high-level abstraction, over knowing what finger goes on what key/fret

text.content: the more low-level customization a product has, the less efficient it is. giving freedom to your users is not about letting me change colors, fonts, etc. it’s about getting the task done. to get a task done, you have to guide their focus to the meaningful, impactful actions. be opinionated about the non-relevant details. liberate users to think about what is relevant. less customization leads to more focus. you want to give focus, not freedom

text.content: neglecting local & physical social relationships is a mistake I’ve made for too long. i feel stuck in parasocial, digital relationships. following people you admire online is great for perspective. but ultimately, in-person relationships create the biggest impact
